refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the customer-defined evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is capable of performing its intended mission to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it explicitly defines the measurement basis (availability ratio), the target threshold (≥0.97), the evaluation period scope, and acknowledges that availability is a system-level emergent property dependent on reliability, maintainability, and safety-driven operational constraints. High safety criticality requires that availability targets not compromise safety; safety-driven shutdowns are legitimate downtime.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events, failure modes, maintenance duration) across the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total time − unplanned downtime − planned maintenance − safety shutdowns) / total time; compare against ≥0.97 threshold; perform root-cause analysis on any shortfalls.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through fault detection and predictive maintenance.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall support rapid recovery from failures via modular design and spare-part availability.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time operational status visibility to enable proactive availability management.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall ensure safety-driven operational constraints do not degrade availability below the target without explicit customer acceptance.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant critical subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual safety circuits) with automatic failover to eliminate single points of failure.
DP-B: Establish a predictive maintenance program using real-time telemetry analytics to detect degradation early and schedule maintenance during planned downtime windows.
DP-C: Design modular, hot-swappable component architecture with standardized interfaces to enable rapid field replacement without full system shutdown.
DP-D: Integrate continuous health monitoring and diagnostics across all subsystems with automated alerts to operators and maintenance teams.
DP-E: Combine redundancy (DP-A), predictive maintenance (DP-B), modular design (DP-C), and health monitoring (DP-D) into a holistic availability management strategy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Implement a holistic availability management strategy combining: (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) predictive maintenance driven by real-time telemetry analytics, (3) modular hot-swappable component architecture with standardized interfaces, and (4) continuous health monitoring with automated diagnostics and alerts. This strategy addresses both reliability (reducing failures) and maintainability (reducing recovery time), the two primary drivers of availability.

potential_sources: Redundancy design (DP-A) must be coordinated with modular architecture (DP-C) to avoid redundant modules becoming tightly coupled.
Predictive maintenance (DP-B) depends on health monitoring (DP-D); these are coupled but in a desirable way (monitoring feeds maintenance decisions).
Failover logic must be decoupled from the primary control path to avoid introducing new failure modes.
coupling_check.notes: DP-E is a composite strategy that integrates four complementary design approaches. The coupling is manageable if each sub-strategy is assigned to distinct architectural layers (redundancy at subsystem level, modularity at component level, monitoring at sensor/telemetry level, maintenance at operational level). No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be implemented without excessive cost/weight/complexity penalties acceptable to customer.
Assumption: Real-time telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network, analytics) is available and reliable.
Assumption: Modular design does not compromise performance or safety margins.
Risk: Redundancy increases system complexity, potentially introducing new failure modes in failover logic.
Risk: Predictive maintenance requires historical failure data; early in product lifecycle, data may be sparse.
Risk: Hot-swappable design may create safety hazards if not carefully managed (e.g., electrical isolation, mechanical locking).
Risk: High safety criticality may require safety-driven shutdowns that conflict with availability targets; trade-off must be explicit.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-E to explicitly separate the availability strategy into four sub-DPs with clear ownership and interface contracts: (1) DP-E.1 (Redundancy): Specify which subsystems are redundant and failover criteria. (2) DP-E.2 (Predictive Maintenance): Define telemetry metrics, analytics algorithms, and maintenance scheduling logic. (3) DP-E.3 (Modularity): Define component boundaries, standardized interfaces, and hot-swap procedures. (4) DP-E.4 (Health Monitoring): Define sensor suite, diagnostic algorithms, and alert thresholds. This decomposition will enable clearer IM assignment and risk allocation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system integration module responsible for orchestrating availability across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). It owns the availability management strategy, including redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance scheduling, modular component integration, and health monitoring aggregation. It is the system-level owner of the ≥0.97 availability KPI.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems providing real-time operational status, sensor data, and health metrics required for predictive maintenance analytics and availability monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound tasking commands from NGHE Solution to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, including maintenance scheduling directives and operational mode adjustments to optimize availability.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety alerts between NGHE Solution and Safety Services; inbound alerts trigger availability-aware operational constraints; outbound alerts communicate system health status relevant to safety decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Network connectivity for telemetry aggregation, predictive maintenance analytics (potentially cloud-based), and coordination with remote monitoring/tele-operation infrastructure.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level owner of the availability KPI. It integrates redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance scheduling, modular design governance, and health monitoring aggregation. Minimal diffusion: availability strategy is centralized at the solution level, with sub-strategies delegated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via clear interfaces.

Vehicle owns redundancy of propulsion/steering subsystems and modular component design, but availability is not solely a Vehicle concern; it depends on Teleoperation reliability, Safety Services constraints, and system-level coordination. Assigning availability KPI to Vehicle alone would create diffusion across subsystems.

Teleoperation contributes to availability through network reliability and failover to autonomous mode, but does not own the full availability strategy. Assigning the KPI here would fragment responsibility.

Safety Services constrains availability (safety-driven shutdowns) but does not own the availability strategy. Assigning the KPI here would create a conflict of interest (safety vs. availability optimization).

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of the availability KPI because: (1) Availability is a system-level emergent property that depends on the integration of Vehicle reliability, Teleoperation robustness, and Safety Services constraints. (2) The availability management strategy (redundancy, predictive maintenance, modularity, health monitoring) requires system-level orchestration and cannot be fully contained within any single subsystem. (3) NGHE Solution owns the interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) necessary to implement the strategy. (4) Assigning the KPI to NGHE Solution creates clear accountability and avoids diffusion across subsystems.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if it attempts to own too much detailed logic. Mitigation: Delegate sub-strategies (redundancy logic, maintenance algorithms, diagnostic rules) to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; NGHE Solution orchestrates via interfaces.

Risk: Availability depends on external factors (customer maintenance practices, environmental conditions, network reliability) outside NGHE Solution control. Mitigation: Clearly define the boundary of NGHE Solution responsibility in the availability target (e.g., 'availability of NGHE-provided subsystems assuming customer follows maintenance plan').

Risk: Safety-driven shutdowns may be mandated by Safety Services, reducing availability below target. Mitigation: Establish explicit trade-off rules in NGHE Solution design (e.g., 'safety always takes precedence; availability target assumes safety shutdowns are rare').
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution IM boundary to explicitly define: (1) Availability ownership scope: NGHE Solution owns the system-level availability KPI and orchestration strategy, but delegates sub-strategy implementation to Vehicle (redundancy, modularity), Teleoperation (failover, network resilience), and Safety Services (safety-aware availability constraints). (2) Interface contracts: Define Telemetry schema for health metrics, Tasking schema for maintenance directives, SafetyAlert schema for availability-safety trade-offs, and Network Link requirements for analytics. (3) Assumptions: Availability target assumes customer follows recommended maintenance plan, environmental conditions within design envelope, and network availability ≥0.99. (4) Allocation: Create sub-FRs for each sub-strategy and allocate to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services in subsequent decomposition levels.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability over evaluation period) is clearly mapped to DP-E (holistic availability management strategy combining redundancy, predictive maintenance, modularity, and health monitoring). The DP directly addresses the FR by reducing unplanned downtime (redundancy, predictive maintenance) and recovery time (modularity, health monitoring). No independence axiom violation: the DP is necessary and sufficient to satisfy the FR. The DP is implementable and measurable.

diffusion_risks: DP-E is a composite strategy with four sub-strategies (redundancy, predictive maintenance, modularity, health monitoring). Risk: These sub-strategies may diffuse across multiple modules if not carefully allocated. Mitigation: Assign each sub-strategy to a specific module in the next decomposition level (e.g., redundancy to Vehicle, predictive maintenance to NGHE Solution, modularity to Vehicle, health monitoring to NGHE Solution).

DP-E depends on real-time telemetry and network connectivity. Risk: If Telemetry and Network Link interfaces are not reliably implemented, the entire availability strategy fails. Mitigation: Treat Telemetry and Network Link as critical infrastructure; allocate high reliability targets to these interfaces.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for DP-E because it owns the system-level orchestration and integration of the four sub-strategies. However, the actual implementation of each sub-strategy will be distributed: Vehicle will implement redundancy and modularity; NGHE Solution will implement predictive maintenance scheduling and health monitoring aggregation; Teleoperation will implement failover logic; Safety Services will define availability-safety trade-offs. The NGHE Solution IM boundary acts as the integration point and KPI owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable: collect operational telemetry over the evaluation period, calculate availability as (total time − unplanned downtime − planned maintenance − safety shutdowns) / total time, and compare against ≥0.97 threshold. Verification requires instrumentation of Telemetry interface to capture uptime/downtime events. Feasibility is high if telemetry infrastructure is in place.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The FR is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution as the system-level owner. Sub-FRs (minimize unplanned downtime, support rapid recovery, provide status visibility, ensure safety-availability trade-offs) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution in subsequent decomposition levels. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-E into four sub-DPs with explicit ownership: DP-E.1 (Redundancy, owned by Vehicle), DP-E.2 (Predictive Maintenance, owned by NGHE Solution), DP-E.3 (Modularity, owned by Vehicle), DP-E.4 (Health Monitoring, owned by NGHE Solution). This will clarify the IM allocation in the next level.

Define explicit interface contracts for Telemetry (health metrics schema), Tasking (maintenance directives schema), SafetyAlert (availability-safety trade-off rules), and Network Link (uptime/latency requirements). These contracts are critical to the availability strategy.

Establish clear assumptions and boundary conditions for the availability target: (1) Evaluation period duration and measurement methodology. (2) Definition of 'operational availability' (does it include planned maintenance? safety shutdowns?). (3) Customer responsibilities (maintenance plan, environmental conditions, network availability). (4) NGHE Solution responsibilities (design, implementation, support). (5) Trade-off rules for safety vs. availability conflicts.

decision.justification: The refined FR, selected DP, and assigned IM are coherent and pass all gate reviews. However, DP-E is a composite strategy with four sub-strategies (redundancy, predictive maintenance, modularity, health monitoring) that require further decomposition to clarify ownership, interfaces, and implementation details. Proceeding directly to implementation without decomposing DP-E would risk diffusion of responsibility and unclear interface contracts. A ZIGZAG_DOWN decision enables systematic decomposition of each sub-strategy into its own FR-DP-IM chain, ensuring clarity and traceability.
decision.justification_detail: DP-E is not atomic; it combines four distinct design approaches that must be decomposed and allocated to specific modules. The next level of decomposition will create four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) and allocate each to a specific DP and IM. This will enable clear ownership, interface contracts, and risk allocation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through redundancy of critical subsystems and automatic failover.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall support rapid recovery from failures through modular design, standardized interfaces, and spare-part availability.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time operational status visibility and predictive maintenance analytics to enable proactive availability management.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall ensure safety-driven operational constraints are managed transparently without degrading availability below the target without explicit customer acceptance.

assumptions: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be implemented without excessive cost/weight/complexity penalties acceptable to customer.
Assumption: Real-time telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network, analytics) is available and reliable (≥0.99 uptime).
Assumption: Modular design does not compromise performance or safety margins.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be developed and validated with available historical failure data.
Assumption: Hot-swappable component design can be implemented safely (electrical isolation, mechanical locking, operator training).
Assumption: Safety-driven shutdowns are rare and do not dominate the availability calculation.
Assumption: Customer will follow recommended maintenance plan and operate within design envelope.
Assumption: Evaluation period is sufficient to capture representative failure modes and maintenance cycles (recommend ≥12 months).
open_risks: Risk: Redundancy increases system complexity, potentially introducing new failure modes in failover logic. Mitigation: Rigorous testing of failover scenarios; formal verification of failover logic.

Risk: Predictive maintenance requires historical failure data; early in product lifecycle, data may be sparse. Mitigation: Use physics-based degradation models and accelerated testing to bootstrap predictive models.
Risk: Hot-swappable design may create safety hazards if not carefully managed. Mitigation: Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) of hot-swap procedures; operator training and certification.

Risk: High safety criticality may require safety-driven shutdowns that conflict with availability targets. Mitigation: Establish explicit trade-off rules; design for 'fail-safe' rather than 'fail-operational' where safety is at stake.

Risk: Network reliability is critical to telemetry and predictive maintenance; network outages directly impact availability. Mitigation: Design for network resilience (local caching, autonomous operation, graceful degradation).

Risk: Availability target (≥0.97) may be unachievable with current technology or cost constraints. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility study; validate target with customer; consider phased implementation.

Risk: Diffusion of availability responsibility across Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution may create accountability gaps. Mitigation: Explicit interface contracts and ownership allocation in next decomposition level.
evidence_needed: Evidence: Feasibility study on redundancy implementation (cost, weight, complexity, performance impact).
Evidence: Telemetry infrastructure design and reliability analysis (sensor suite, network architecture, analytics platform).
Evidence: Modular component design specifications and hot-swap procedure documentation.
Evidence: Predictive maintenance algorithm design and validation plan (data sources, model selection, accuracy targets).
Evidence: Safety analysis of redundancy and hot-swap procedures (FMEA, FTA, hazard log).
Evidence: Interface contracts for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link (schema, uptime requirements, latency requirements).
Evidence: Availability measurement methodology and evaluation plan (data collection, calculation, reporting).
Evidence: Customer acceptance of availability target and assumptions (signed requirements document).
